item.title: This Pro-Israel Group Paved the Way for Trump’s Disastrous War in Iran
item.description: Last June, Tzvi Kahn published a list of 44 reported Iranian attacks against Americans. Kahn, a former employee of the pro-Israel lobby AIPAC, had written the list to illustrate that “Tehran continues to pose a threat to U.S. forces in the Middle East, the American homeland, and Americans residing…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Last June, Tzvi Kahn published a list of 44 reported Iranian attacks against Americans. Kahn, a former employee of the pro-Israel lobby AIPAC, had written the list to illustrate that “Tehran continues to pose a threat to U.S. forces in the Middle East, the American homeland, and Americans residing across the globe.”

Eight months later, the White House published the exact same list, almost word for word, using it to justify President Donald Trump’s new joint war with Israel on Iran.

Kahn’s list was originally published by the Foundation for the Defense of Democracies (FDD), a pro-Israel thank tank funded by some of the same billionaires who have dumped millions into U.S. elections to keep pro-peace candidates out of Congress.

The White House’s apparent copy-and-pasting of the FDD list is a symbol for how intertwined the tenaciously pro-Israel FDD is with the Trump administration – and consequently how pro-war the administration really is.

A closer look inside FDD shows how it has served as a revolving door between the U.S. and Israeli government. It also reveals how it has long planted the seeds for Trump’s disastrous regime-change war with Iran – which has killed thousands, including U.S. troops, is and throttling the global economy, with no end in sight.

And a paper trail suggests that FDD may even have something to do with developments as recently as this week to fuse the Israeli and U.S. militaries.

FDD was founded originally by a New York Times journalist-turned-Republican staffer in April 2001, and originally known as EMET (the Hebrew word for “truth”). At the time, the organization’s stated purpose was “to provide education to enhance Israel’s image in North America.”

After 9/11, the group changed its name to FDD, expanding its stated focus; since then, FDD and its allies have insisted it is a nonpartisan policy institute focused on security combating extremism.

Early on, it was a bipartisan affair. The organization’s top advisors ranged from then-leading neoconservative Bill Kristol and former Republican House Speaker Newt Gingrich and former directors of the FBI and CIA, to Democratic Senators Chuck Schumer and Joe Lieberman and future DNC chair Donna Brazile (who famously leaked debate topics to Hillary Clinton during the 2016 Democratic primary).

As the organization became increasingly more partisan, the Democrats would eventually leave their leadership roles.

Right off the bat, the group benefited from massive inflows of cash from prominent right-wing donors including Home Depot co-founder Bernard Marcus; right-wing hedge fund manager Paul Singer; casino magnate Sheldon Adelson, who was a personal friend of Benjamin Netanyahu, and whose Trump megadonor wife, Miriam, has continued dumping millions into pro-Israel efforts; and Democratic donor Haim Saban (who would later attack the Biden administration for even considering blocking a weapons shipment to Israel amid its genocide in Gaza).

FDD has been jockeying for years to get what Trump has given them: an outright regime-change war with Iran.

The group also staunchly opposed the nuclear agreement during the Obama administration, and has worked to ensure Iran faces the most stringent sanctions possible.

During the first Trump administration, FDD CEO Mark Dubowitz sent a memo to Trump’s national security council, exploring how to incite unrest in Iran with the goal of “coerced democratization,” as the memo put it. “The very structure of the regime invites instability, crisis and possibly collapse,” it argued.

After Trump ripped up Obama’s nuclear deal and enacted a policy of “maximum pressure,” FDD celebrated: “Two Years On, the Trump Administration’s Iran Policy Continues to Make Sense.”

Of course, Trump wasn’t solely “dragged” into his illegal war in Iran by an outside force, let alone one single think tank. Trump’s threats against Iran span back to his first term, and it’s not as if the upper echelons of Republican politics are in wanting of war hawks or pro-Israel predilection. Still, FDD has unusually strong influence.

“FDD is one of the most active think tanks in DC in terms of its engagement with the Executive Branch in my experience,” says Josh Paul, who resigned from the State Department in 2023 in protest over Israel’s genocide in Gaza. He adds that FDD “has played a role for both Republican and Democratic administrations both as a contributor to foreign policy and as a means of communicating policy to the American people.”

Along with taking credit for Trump’s “maximum pressure” posture toward Iran, and his shredding of Obama’s nuclear deal, FDD has helped lead the fight to protect Israel by placing sanctions on its enemies.

At a 2019 AIPAC conference, FDD CEO Mark Dubowitz thanked the pro-Israel lobby for helping pass a bill enacting sanctions against Hamas and Hezbollah for supposedly using civilians as human shields. In effect, the law is meant to punish those groups when Israel kills civilians. And it doesn’t apply to Israel, which has been documented as actively using Palestinians as human shields.

Speaking on a panel, Dubowitz said the bill is designed to, in part, boost Israeli PR. “This legislation, its most important effect, again, is from a strategic and communications point of view,” he said. “It is to shift the focus from Israel, which the international community believes is responsible for war crimes, to those who are really responsible for the war crimes. That’s Hezbollah, Hamas, and other terrorist organizations using civilians as human shields.”

Even a 2019 Atlantic piece arguing the focus on FDD’s influence is overblown admits: “no place else has made an institutional specialty of recommending hard-line Iran policies and offering detailed proposals for how to implement them the way the FDD has done.”

That same year, when Iran blacklisted FDD – putting it on its own sanctions list and accusing it of economic terrorism for its role in designing and advocating sanctions against Iran – Secretary of State Mike Pompeo ran to its defense.

“Continuing attempts by the regime in Iran to intimidate @FDD, an American think tank – a THINK TANK – must be condemned by all freedom-loving people around the world,” Pompeo tweeted. “The U.S. does not take these threats lightly, and will hold the regime and its “apparatuses” to account.”

The administration’s copy-and-pasting of Kahn’s list is not an isolated incident; the administration has been accused of publishing materials closely mirroring FDD messaging in years past. This included the first Trump administration issuing statements that had notable overlap with FDD messaging.

Cavan Kharrazian, international program researcher for the Center for Economic and Policy Research, told In These Times: “If you read the statements coming from Trump’s State Department on Iran and the ‘maximum pressure’ campaign, they appear almost completely ideologically aligned with these think tanks’ own positions and talking points, especially during the Covid-19 crisis.”

And along with Kahn’s list of 44 Iranian attacks, last month, the White House used an FDD graphic to support Trump’s justifications for his war.

Some bristled at the use of the graphic, like Joe Kent, who resigned as Trump’s top counterterrorism official in protest of Trump’s war in Iran.

“The U.S. IC assessed that Iran was not seeking a nuclear weapon, yet unverified information from pro-Israel groups dominated the intelligence reaching POTUS in the lead-up to the war,” Kent tweeted in response to the White House’s use of the graphic. “Groups like FDD, members of the media, donors, and key Israeli officials played a key role in convincing Trump that his policy should shift to zero enrichment and that the Iranian regime would quickly crumble if he struck now.”

There’s also the matter of the revolving door. Bonnie Glick, a FDD adjunct senior fellow, served as Trump’s deputy administrator for the U.S. Agency for International Development.

FDD senior advisor Richard Goldberg held a litany of government roles – including director for countering Iranian weapons of mass destruction for the White House National Security Council. His FDD profile notes that, in Trump’s first term, Goldberg “helped coordinate key elements of President Trump’s maximum pressure campaign against Iran.”

FDD advisor Charles Kupperman was Trump’s deputy national security advisor. H.R. McMaster, who was Trump’s national security advisor, is chairman of the board at FDD’s center on military and political power.

And just last month, as Trump’s “negotiations” with Iran continued to falter, he recruited Nick Stewart, the managing director of advocacy at FDD’s lobbying arm to join Steve Witkoff’s diplomatic team. FDD Action, which has spent $1.1 million on lobbying efforts since last year, says it provides private briefings, trainings, and assistance drafting bills free of charge to government staffers and officials.

The revolving door in Washington is not novel, but the closeness between the Trump administration and FDD is notable. It’s all the more important, given that there is another concurrent funnel: Israeli officials to FDD.

There’s FDD senior fellow Jonathan Conricus, a former Israeli military commander and Israeli spokesperson. Conricus served as an Israeli spokesperson at the start of Israel’s last genocidal war in Gaza; his tenure included hand-holding journalists through al-Shifa Hospital, purporting to show materials that justified Israel’s bombing of it (a practice FDD has similarly pursued).

FDD senior fellow Jacob Nagel served as head of Israel’s national security council and as Netanyahu’s national security advisor. “He led the negotiations and signed the agreement for 38 billion dollar military aid to Israel from 2018 to 2027,” FDD’s website boasts. Prior to that, he led an Israel team working on negotiations for the Iran nuclear deal. And before that, Nagel led the “Nagel Committee,” which advanced the recommendation to develop the Iron Dome. Prior to that, he served in the Israeli military.

Last year, FDD welcomed to its ranks Tal Kelman, a retired Israeli general.

Among the more egregious examples is Merav Ceren, a former Israeli Ministry for Defense staffer who worked as a FDD fellow during Trump’s first administration. Last year, she took on a role as director for Israel and Iran at the U.S. National Security Council.

FDD has maintained open warm relations with Israel. In 2024, it hosted internationally wanted war criminal and former Israeli Defense Minister Yoav Gallant.

The porousness between the Trump-Vance administration, the Israeli government, and FDD altogether lead to a closed loop of militant individuals – backed by the same prominent pro-Israel funders – hyper-focused on goals attuned more to Israeli interests than American ones.

Andrew Cockburn, a national security author and Washington Editor of Harper’s Magazine recounted this week: “A former CIA director described FDD to me as the ‘special forces of the DC think tank community.’ Horrifyingly effective.”

Alongside its possible influence on Trump’s joint war on Iran with Israel, FDD has wider aims.

Last year, FDD published a memo calling for a “United States-Israel Strategic Partnership Agreement.” It’s framed as a successor to the current memorandum of understanding that sends $3.8 billion in U.S. tax dollars to Israel each year.

FDD frames this partnership agreement as mutually beneficial, a symbiotic path forward that would have Israel guaranteeing purchases from the U.S. – creating American jobs by “an injection of Israeli resources” – and supporting joint U.S.-Israeli defense research and development. A way to strengthen the U.S. defense base, ensure deep ties between the nations, and yes, continue arming Israel.

The roadmap is timely. As more and more Democrats catch up to public opinion and call for ending U.S. aid to Israel, Netanyahu and hawkish lawmakers like Senator Lindsey Graham are scrambling to get ahead of it, by themselves saying Israel should taper off U.S. military funding.

By doing so, they are attempting to steer a conversation that’s veering out of their control, taking it back toward an end that remains desirable for Israel.

The agreement, Eli Clifton – co-founder of the Quincy Institute and co-author of the forthcoming book, Israel’s Lobby: America in the Grip of a Foreign Power – argues “would tie the U.S. defense industries and military to Israel in a manner that would be extremely difficult to separate as time passes.”

“In short, with 60% of Americans now holding unfavorable views toward Israel, Israel’s lobby, via FDD and other groups, is actively rushing to create institutional architecture tying the two countries together in ways that will be extremely difficult to undo,” Clifton told Zeteo.

And the 2025 memo is already maturating inside Congress. Buried in the Republican-led House version of the 2027 Pentagon budget is what’s called the “United States-Israel Defense Technology Cooperation Initiative,” a measure that seeks to integrate U.S. and Israeli military sectors. The measure, section 224 of the NDAA, would have the U.S. and Israeli militaries co-produce weapons, expand coordination in the realms of AI and biotechnology, and share U.S. military data with Israel.

The fusing of the militaries would have cascading effects. As Ben Freeman of Responsible Statecraft writes: “It would give the Israeli government the opportunity to greatly expand one of the most powerful levers of influence in U.S. politics: jobs in the U.S. By expanding or starting new co-production facilities like it already has in Mississippi and Arkansas, the Israeli government could boast of providing jobs on U.S. soil, thereby securing allies among members of Congress who represent the districts where those jobs lie.”

Paul, formerly of the State Department and founder of A New Policy, said that he believes Israel probably preferred the framework of the 2025 FDD memo – which would have the US still provide $5 billion to Israel each year (all to be spent in the US). But, with how politics have shifted since, such a financial commitment doesn’t fly any more. “Peel that off, and what you’re left with is the concept that underlies NDAA Sec. 224, which is what they are now trying to codify in order to cut their losses,” Paul said.

Meanwhile, Netanyahu himself seems to indeed be actively championing such a shift. On Monday, he reportedly sent a note to Indiana Republican Rep. Marlin Stutzman, who introduced a resolution to end the $3.8 billion aid to Israel, and instead move towards “mutual defense cooperation and joint economic investment.”

“I was glad to receive your proposed congressional resolution endorsing my plan to shift the framework for U.S.-Israel defense cooperation from aid to partnership,” Netanyahu wrote.

“The time has now arrived for us to move from aid recipient to partner,” he continued. “I am heartened by your enthusiastic support for our plan to develop a new Memorandum of Understanding with the United States government that will draw down U.S. financial military assistance over the next decade and replace it with what you refer to as a “new framework of joint defense cooperation, codevelopment, coproduction and mutual investment in areas including advanced missile defense, artificial intelligence unmanned systems, cybersecurity and next generation military platforms.”

That’s almost exactly what FDD proposed last year – and now it has become both a Republican and Israeli policy proposal.

In the meantime, though, FDD remains focused on Iran. This week, Dubowitz, FDD’s CEO, insisted: “If the goal is to cripple the Iranian regime, we are closer today than at any point in 47 years. The opportunity is real, but so is the risk of squandering it.”

Speaking of an Iran war that the administration has long pledged would be brief, Dubowitz added: “President Trump will need the strategic patience and staying power to finish the job over the next two and a half years.”

Have a tip for Prem? Text him on Signal @ premthakker.35 — you can remain anonymous

item.title: Inside ‘Coward’ Bari Weiss’s ‘Shitshow’ at ‘60 Minutes’
item.description: Good afternoon, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In today’s special edition, we dive into the five-alarm fire that is the current state of ‘60 Minutes’ and CBS News…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Good afternoon, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In today’s special edition, we dive into the five-alarm fire that is the current state of ‘60 Minutes’ and CBS News after Scott Pelley was ousted for dressing down Bari Weiss’s handpicked executive producer of the show while accusing her of “murdering” the network. Strap in for a slew of exclusive details and insights about this latest Bari trainwreck…

“I’m not answering that question.”

That’s what Scott Pelley says Bari Weiss told him during Tuesday night’s acrimonious meeting, which saw the veteran correspondent get summarily fired, when he pressed her on why she had terminated the senior staff of ‘60 Minutes.’

The ousting of Pelley from ‘60 Minutes,’ as insiders tell me, appears to serve as the depressing climax of Bari Weiss’s reign of terror over CBS News, which has seen the “anti-woke crusader” and her “band of moronic misfits” run roughshod over the Tiffany Network in her attempt to bend the news organization to its knees.

All the while, as she looks to remake it in the image of her center-right, pro-Israel blog The Free Press, the network under Weiss’s leadership has seen a slew of negative headlines amid a series of screwups, poor ratings, and accusations of meddling on her part. And now she’s served perhaps a fatal reputational blow to CBS’s most iconic brand.

In fact, Pelley – who had spent more than 20 years as a ‘60 Minutes’ correspondent – echoed a refrain that has become all too familiar during Weiss’s chaotic tenure at CBS News.

“For my part, new management has instructed me to inject falsehoods and bias into a politically sensitive story,” Pelley noted in his Tuesday evening statement about the firing. “I’ve been told to include assertions that are unverified. To date, in every case, I have managed to ignore these instructions or refuse them.”

These same allegations have been made by Cecilia Vega and Sharyn Alfonsi, who were fired last week as ‘60 Minutes’ correspondents.

“In recent months, my producing teams and I have experienced efforts to insert political bias into our stories,” Vega said after she was let go.

Alfonsi, whose “Inside CECOT” story was abruptly pulled from the air by Weiss because the editor-in-chief wanted more Trump voices added, said her firing was “a deliberate choice to penalize a journalist for refusing to sanitize factually accurate reporting.”

At the top of Wednesday morning’s editorial meeting, Weiss addressed the elephant in the room, according to an audio recording obtained by Zeteo.

“I need to address what’s transpired in our newsroom over the past two days and what is making news. I hope I speak – I know I speak for myself, and I hope I speak for everyone here when I say that I’m only interested in working in a newsroom that is built on trust and mutual respect. We cannot do our work without it,” Weiss, who has long portrayed herself as an anti-cancel culture warrior, declared. “That foundation was broken on Monday, and despite our attempts to engage with Scott Pelley and to find a way back, unfortunately, we weren’t able to do so, and so we had to part ways. We did not want that to happen, but that’s the path that he chose.”

Shortly after the editorial call, Pelley disputed Weiss’s claims that management had tried to “find a way back” for him to remain at CBS following Monday’s heated clash with Nick Bilton, Weiss’s handpicked new executive producer of ‘60 Minutes.’

Stating that Weiss refused to answer his questions about the mass firings, Pelley said the CBS executives were “abrupt, dismissive and uninterested in dialogue” before angrily showing him the door. “These executives cannot gain the trust of staff with lies,” he noted.

Network sources told me that Pelley was urged to apologize for supposedly creating a hostile work environment during his confrontation with Bilton.

“For an anti-woke crusader, this is pretty woke of [Weiss] to accuse Pelley of after firing the entire leadership of his show and two correspondent colleagues,” one CBS News senior staffer told me about the hostile work environment claim.

The latest blow to ‘60 Minutes,’ the venerable newsmagazine that has been appointment television for nearly six decades, has CBS News staffers – past and present – apoplectic over the damage Weiss has wrought on the network and its most prestigious brand, whose remaining staff has been left utterly demoralized.

Sources told me that it’s expected that much of the rank-and-file ‘60 Minutes’ staff would soon be looking for the exits following last week’s mass firings and the termination of Pelley, who was a beloved and iconic figure at the network.

“Secretly, I think there are some of us who would like to say the quiet part out loud directly to Bari Weiss and her contrarian group of know-nothing deputies. Scott did that for us. She’s killing CBS News without any remorse,” a CBS News reporter texted me last night as Pelley learned he had been fired.

“It’s all so depressing,” the senior network staffer noted on Wednesday.

CBS News did not immediately respond to a request for comment

Rather than outright deliver the news of his firing to Pelley herself, Weiss instead leaned on Bilton – who, like Weiss, is a former New York Times columnist with no prior experience in broadcast television news – to email the legendary reporter that he’d been terminated for cause.

“Yesterday, you hijacked my first meeting with staff to disparage me,” Bilton wrote, adding: “Your antipathy to the future of the show has come through loud and clear. And I have heard you. I therefore write on behalf of CBS News, Inc. to inform you that your employment with CBS is terminated for cause effective immediately.” (It has been speculated that this will launch a legal challenge by Pelley.)

The pink slip came a day after Pelley torched Bilton and Weiss – who was notably absent – during a contentious ‘60 Minutes’ staff meeting, which saw Pelley accuse Weiss of “murdering” the show while running down Bilton for having “slender qualifications.”

That meeting, which only ran for about 15 minutes and saw Bilton bolt out after saying he wouldn’t be “intimidated” by Pelley, came just days after Weiss fired Alfonsi, Vega, executive producer Tanya Simon, and much of the senior production staff.

“I find it odd that you would take this job knowing that you would never be welcomed here,” Pelley pointedly told Bilton at one point in the contentious Monday meeting, pointing out how “cruel” last week’s firings were, which he described as “Black Thursday.”

He also shot back at Charles Forelle – one of Weiss’s top deputies who was in the meeting – after he was accused of being “rude” to Bilton. “Not talking about Tanya’s contract. Not talking about Sharyn Alfonsi’s contract. Not talking about Cecilia Vega’s contract,” Pelley exclaimed. “Just calling them up and telling they were fired. That’s rude. This is a conversation. That is rude, and you were part of that.”

Following that dramatic showdown, according to Status’s Oliver Darcy, Pelley – who had turned down prior efforts by Weiss and Bilton to speak about his future following the mass firings – came to CBS offices on Tuesday armed with a resignation letter. Meanwhile, Weiss and Bilton spent the day weighing whether to fire him or find a path forward with Pelley remaining on the show.

After the acrimonious late-afternoon meeting, which featured Bilton, Weiss, and CBS News President Tom Cibrowski, Pelley told his colleagues that he expected to be fired.

By 9 pm ET, his termination was official – which prompted an outcry from CBS News staff, many of whom had applauded him on Monday for telling off his new boss.

“I’ve never seen so many past and present CBS News folks texting and calling about this. This has struck a major nerve,” a network insider told me. According to the insider, network veterans and staffers described the situation as “madness,” “heartbreaking,” and “WTF?!?!”

“Anger and sincere loathing at management, especially Bari, Nick, and Tom Cibrowski,” the insider added.

“At some point, everyone needs to draw a line in the sand. Your family members are watching. Your industry is watching too. Scott did what only a few others have managed to do,” a former CBS reporter said immediately after the firing.

Calling it a “shitshow,” a former CBS News senior staffer labeled Weiss a “coward” for not attending the Monday ‘60 Minutes’ meeting or Tuesday morning editorial call, all while having Bilton email over a termination letter to Pelley.

“Bari just seems to keep doubling down and plowing on. She has this thing about not listening to what she calls the ‘mob,’” the ex-senior staffer said. “Neither Bari nor her people have the management skills to deal with this, and I expect HR and legal teams are sick of it.”

There are essentially three camps among CBS staffers regarding what’s become of the network under Weiss, the CBS News reporter explained.

“There are those who make enough money to ignore the mismanagement of the news division to the point that they are burying their heads in the sand for a fat paycheck,” the reporter declared. “Then, there are those who are so ethically compromised that they are publicly congratulatory toward her and everyone she’s hired. Perhaps it’s survival mode, or perhaps it’s something more sinister – like blind ambition. You can see those people publicly kissing ass.”

“And then, there are those of us who are absolutely disgusted by what David Ellison has unleashed on us in the form of Bari Weiss and her ilk of cronies, friends, and family members who she is installing as our overlords,” the reporter added. “Those of us who still have journalistic values are cheering Scott Pelley on – as were his 60 colleagues who applauded during this meeting.”

In fact, according to CNN’s Brian Stelter, Ellison ultimately signed off on Pelley’s firing following the correspondent’s condemnation of both Bilton and Weiss at Monday’s meeting.

In his statement about his firing, Pelley plainly and directly accused Ellison – the owner of CBS who is currently in the middle of a mega-merger with Warner Bros. Discovery – of revamping the network and ‘60 Minutes’ to appease Donald Trump. (Let us not forget that it was Trump’s lawsuit against ‘60 Minutes’ that led us down the road to this moment.)

Pelley also said that the Weiss regime had tried to politically meddle with his stories while allowing politicians “to choose correspondents for interviews on the broadcast,” referencing Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu negotiating with Weiss to have Major Garrett interview him for ‘60 Minutes’ rather than Lesley Stahl, who had been trying to land a sit-down for months. He added that the “incompetence and unprofessionalism in the new management have wreaked havoc.”

That sentiment is shared by media experts and industry insiders I spoke to, who warned that Weiss’s already “disastrous” tenure atop CBS News had now truly jumped the shark with the mess she’d created around the network’s top franchise.

“It’s one cluster after another, and there’s no thought going into any of this. It’s spaghetti on the wall… and it’s running roughshod over everything, instead of sitting there thinking strategically,” one television executive told me.

“Each decision that she makes is worse than the last. I don’t understand it. And what are you going to do? You’re going to fire Scott Pelley,” the executive continued, noting that the likelihood is that ‘60 Minutes’ would soon lose veteran correspondents Lesley Stahl and Bill Whitaker, leaving the show with just one full-time correspondent ahead of next season.

Meanwhile, during Wednesday morning’s editorial meeting, Weiss attempted to reassure staff that, despite the loss of four correspondents (Anderson Cooper called it quits after this season) and the show’s leadership, things would be just fine under Bilton amid this latest self-imposed disaster.

Positively referencing some of Pelley’s reporting, Weiss said, “Those are the kind of stories that have always, always typified ‘60 Minutes.’” She then pivoted to hyping Bilton’s vision for the show in the upcoming season, asserting that he’d continue the show’s legacy with the “amazing team that’s still there” and “new people” that he’d hire.

Columbia Journalism School professor Bill Grueskin told me that Bilton and Weiss will almost certainly have an extremely difficult time securing buy-in from current and new staffers for the changes they are proposing.

“While a new, change-driven boss sometimes has to clear out longtime employees to make the reform they need, at a certain point you have to lay out a coherent, optimistic -- and detailed -- vision for how you plan to turn things around,” Grueskin said

“Bari Weiss has yet to do that,” he added.

Grueskin added that “‘60 Minutes’ is in big trouble” as “we have seen this happen before, where a new executive with limited relevant experience or management expertise barges in with the goal to engineer a massive transformation that they hope will significantly improve the business and journalism fundamentals.” Indeed, one need only look at what has recently happened with Will Lewis at the Washington Post or

In the end, some folks at CBS are just begging for Ellison to finally pull the plug on Weiss and press reset.

“I think there’s a way out of this. But it’s a long shot. It would require undoing so much damage that has really shaken the organization to its core,” the network reporter said. “That kind of restoration cannot happen without removing Bari Weiss and her band of moronic misfits.”

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Hamawy Wins! The Witness to the Genocide in Gaza Is on His Way to Congress
item.description: On this day in 1989, Chinese authorities began their crackdown on pro-democracy protesters in Tiananmen Square, in Beijing. By nightfall the following day, hundreds had been killed and thousands arrested. Donald Trump would later praise the Chinese government for putting down the protests “with…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1989, Chinese authorities began their crackdown on pro-democracy protesters in Tiananmen Square, in Beijing. By nightfall the following day, hundreds had been killed and thousands arrested. Donald Trump would later praise the Chinese government for putting down the protests “with strength.”

Good morning! Andrew here, attempting to recover from one of the more rapid-fire news days we’ve seen in a while – and I cover Donald Trump for a living. If that reads like a plea for help, well, what can I say?

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ an American doctor who treated patients in Gaza just won his Democratic primary race. Plus, Trump gives one of his worst henchmen a new role; the U.S.-Iran war is still not over; and the acting AG says the president’s $1.8 billion slush fund won’t move forward – just don’t put that in writing or anything like that. Seriously.

In April 2024, Zeteo announced its formal launch with a live event at the Spy Museum in DC, featuring Mehdi interviewing Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez on stage. Sitting in the audience that night was a little-known doctor from New Jersey who told Mehdi that he was on his way to volunteer in Gaza.

Last night, that doctor, Adam Hamawy, won the Democratic primary in New Jersey’s 12th congressional district, despite an aggressive smear campaign by pro-Israel Dems, and is now on his way to Congress, where he will presumably join Ocasio-Cortez’s leftist ‘Squad.’

Hamawy easily defeated second-place finisher Brad Cohen, who decried “his association” with terrorists – a reference to Hamawy’s testimony at the trial of Islamist militant Omar Abdel-Rahman in 1995. What his critics don’t mention is that Hamawy was a first-responder in New York on 9/11, treating victims of the terror attack on the Twin Towers, then deployed to Iraq in 2004 and 2005 as a combat medic, and later rose to the rank of lieutenant-colonel in the U.S. Army. Speaking with Zeteo’s Prem Thakker on the eve of the primary, Hamawy called the attacks on him

Two years ago, Hamawy witnessed that genocide up close. During a medical mission, he was trapped in Gaza for weeks, after Israel closed the Rafah border crossing. He told Prem the genocide is “much more horrible in person” than what the world sees on TV and social media. “Until you are physically there and can see with your own eyes and hear with your own ears, you really don’t understand what’s happening,” he said.

Hamawy is credited with saving Democratic Senator Tammy Duckworth’s life during the Iraq War in 2004, and she later went on to help ensure his evacuation from Gaza.

As a congressional candidate, Hamawy became an unlikely frontrunner in the race for New Jersey’s 12th district, racking up endorsements from Senators Duckworth and Bernie Sanders, and progressive Reps. Ocasio-Cortez and Ilhan Omar.

Hamawy also saw support from the progressive group Justice Democrats, and benefited from $1.6 million in spending from a super PAC called American Priorities, a group recently launched as a counterweight to the pro-Israel lobby AIPAC.

“Tonight, voters in New Jersey’s 12th congressional district sent a clear message: They want representatives who are independent, not tied to foreign interests, and willing to speak honestly about American foreign policy,” American Priorities said in a statement.

Hamawy might represent AIPAC’s worst nightmare – a progressive future lawmaker who bore witness to Israel’s genocide in Gaza. Though AIPAC has blanketed the airwaves in other states, it doesn’t appear to have done so against Hamawy.

Progressives, in fact, had a spending advantage in the race. And while a moderate, party-aligned super PAC spent $400,000 to boost Sue Altman, she ended up a distant fifth.

The smears against Hamawy were left to right-wing outlets like the New York Post and Democratic politicians aligned with Israel.

New Jersey Democratic Rep. Josh Gottheimer said of Hamawy: “I have serious questions and deep concerns about his associations with terrorist organizations and leaders who have attacked America.”

Speaking to Prem, Hamawy called Gottheimer “an AIPAC bot,” adding: “I’m glad that I will hopefully get elected and be in office and be able to stand up to this bully.” He should have a chance to do so soon.

In other primary night news:

  • Josh Turek easily

    defeatedZach Wahls in the Iowa Democratic Senate primary. Turek was favored by Schumer and Democratic leadership and benefited from $10 million in spending from VoteVets, while Wahls was endorsed by progressive Senator Elizabeth Warren.

  • The California governor

    racewas too early to call Wednesday morning, but Republican Steve Hilton and moderate Biden alum Xavier Becerra appeared likely to advance to the general election, with billionaire “class traitor”Tom Steyerleft in the cold after spending $213 million.

  • Los Angeles Mayor Karen Bass

    advancedto the November runoff, but it was still too early Wednesday morning to determine who she will face, with former reality TV star Spencer Pratt leading progressive Nithya Raman.

  • As of early Wednesday, the Montana 1st congressional

    racehasn’t been called, with Sam Forstag, who was backed by Sanders and AOC, holding a small lead over Ryan Busse.

  • California’s 22nd congressional race, too early to call Wednesday morning,

    showedSanders-backed Randy Villegas with a small lead over Jasmeet Bains, the party’s pick to challenge incumbent GOP Rep. David Valadao. During the campaign, Bainswalkedback her previous comments acknowledging that Israel has committed genocide in Gaza, and saw backing from the pro-Israel super PAC Democratic Majority for Israel.
  • Rubio ruminates:Secretary of State Marco Rubiotoldsenators “the war is over” but also said a deal could come “today, it could happen tomorrow, it could happen next week” or it might not come at all. Rubio also said reopening the Strait of Hormuz and confronting Iran’s nuclear ambitions were vital to the U.S., but Tehran would not get sanctions relief.
  • New attacks:Later, the U.S. militarysaidIran launched drones and missiles at Bahrain, Kuwait, and civilian shipping, while U.S. strikes hit a military facility on Qeshm Island. Iran said it acted in response. At least one was killed when strikes hit Kuwait’s international airport, causing significant damage, state media said.
  • Lebanon latest:Eleven people werekilledin Israeli strikes in southern Lebanon on Tuesday, a day after Trump said he had secured an agreement to reduce fighting between Israel and Hezbollah. At least six, including two paramedics, were killed in Israeli strikes in the Tyre district today. One strike hit near a hospital. Nearly 3,500 people have been killed and nearly 10,600 injured by Israeli strikes since March, the Lebanese Health Ministrysaid.
  • Heartland heartburn:GOPRep. Ashley Hinson, Trump’s pick for Senate in Iowa, told supporters the Iran war would be “a political liability” if it drags on much longer,Politicoscooped. Hinson also said, “We’ve lost Iowa soldiers. I’ve been to four funerals since December, it’s awful.”

It was a marker for the depth to which the United States has sunk under Donald Trump that when reporters asked about the ludicrous appointment of 38-year-old Freddie Mac/Fannie Mae CEO-cum-MAGA warrior Bill Pulte as acting director of national intelligence, behind the White House podium stood not a press secretary but a TV doctor and shamed fat pill salesman.

“You’re asking the question with the premise that Bill Pulte is not qualified,” said Mehmet Oz, director of the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services, prepping to dodging the question.

Well, yes.

The consensus on Capitol Hill and in the media was that Pulte is not remotely qualified for a role he’ll fill while keeping his others. Perhaps more concerning still is his track record of using questionable tactics to help Trump mount hypocritical, flimsy, political prosecutions of the president’s perceived enemies, including New York AG Letitia James, suggesting looming danger concerning intelligence and the uses to which it is put.

Among Republicans, Senate Majority Leader John Thune said, “We don’t need a weaponized DNI. We need professionals there. If they nominate him to take the position permanently, he’ll have to go through a confirmation process and hearings and everything else, so we’ll see.”

Thune might’ve considered that the office has already been weaponized under Trump: Remember when ex-DNI Tulsi Gabbard was present as FBI agents seized election records in Georgia?

Among Democrats, Senator Mark Warner, vice-chair of the Intelligence Committee, told Meidas Touch that Pulte “will have access to some of our most classified projects, where we try to counter the Russians, Chinese, Iranians, terrorists… just being sloppy, he could reveal things that would put our country at risk.

“I woke up this morning… I didn’t think Donald Trump could still surprise me. Today, [I was] surprised, shocked, [and made] inappropriate comments I can’t make on TV. I’ve been talking a lot, begging my Republican friends. I think we could maybe still get this reversed.”

  • J6 hire:The Pentagon hired a January 6 rioter to work in an office that manages highly classified operations, theWashington Postreported. Elias Irizarry, who was 19 when he entered the Capitol, pleaded guilty to a misdemeanor and was sentenced to 14 days in jail. Trump pardoned him last year.
  • Scott freed:Longtime ‘60 Minutes’ reporter Scott Pelley wasfiredby new executive producer Nick Bilton a day after saying CBS News editor-in-chief Bari Weiss was out to “murder” the show to please owner and Trump ally David Ellison. Pelleyrespondedin a statement, “For my part, new management has instructed me to inject falsehoods and bias into a politically sensitive story.”
  • Map rule:The Supreme CourtallowedAlabama to erase one of two majority Black districts in time for the midterms, another blow to Democrats’ hopes.
  • Out of order:DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin would not commit to following court orders. Senator Chris Murphysaid: “If you’re a Republican or a Democrat on this committee, you should be really, really freaked out.”
  • Maine event:Facingquestions about scandal-plagued Maine progressive Graham Platner, whom he and top Dems met on Tuesday, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer repeated: “We’re gonna beat Susan Collins and take back the Senate.” Platnerreportedlyleft DC early after theNew York Postshowed up at his mother’s restaurant and in-laws’ home.
  • Name game:Alaska GOP Senator Dan Sullivan threatened legal action against theother Dan Sullivanin his re-election race,claimingDemocrats “orchestrated this on purpose to confuse Alaskans” and “rig” the election for Mary Peltola.

Donald Trump’s $1.776 billion “Anti-Weaponization Fund” is no more, acting Attorney General Todd Blanche claimed.

“We’re not moving forward with the fund, period,” Blanche told a House committee.

The fund was meant to pay Trump supporters, including those who attacked Congress on Jan. 6, 2021. The outrage it stoked fueled a rare Republican rebellion, jeopardizing what remains of the Trump legislative agenda – i.e., funding draconian immigration policy. On Tuesday, that effort was back on track, even though Blanche said his equally corrupt addendum to the Anti-Weaponization Fund, the granting of immunity from Internal Revenue Service investigation for Trump, his family, and his businesses, would remain in place. Democrats are preparing a challenge to that.

Regarding the slush fund, Blanche would not commit “to putting anything in writing.” He also said “the reasons for the fund... remain as important as [they] were before,” being to pay off supposed victims of “weaponized” law enforcement, such as Trump claims to be.

In messages to a PBS correspondent, former Proud Boys leader Enrique Tarrio, convicted of seditious conspiracy and then pardoned by Trump, wrote that even without the fund, “the president will find a way [to pay out]… They can just settle the tort claims and lawsuits. That has no judicial review or congressional oversight. And it would mean a lot more money in compensation.”

On Tuesday, Donald Trump claimed to have “built” the Waldorf Astoria hotel in DC. How many men employed on the actual 1890s construction of what was then the General Post Office might yet come back to haunt Trump, having been killed during the project?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Gaza health officialssaidat least four people were killed in separate Israeli strikes on Tuesday. More than 930 Palestinians have been killed by Israel since October, when a supposed ceasefire went into effect.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    Ukraine assault:At least 22 were killed, and 130 wounded in Russian missile and drone attacks on cities including Kyiv and Dnipro, Ukrainesaid. President Volodymyr Zelensky said he expected further attacks.

  • 🇧🇩🇲🇲

    Aid warning:The United Nationssaidcuts in global aid led by the U.S. could severely worsen conditions for 1.2 million Rohingya Muslim refugees who fled Myanmar for Bangladesh.
  • 🇿🇼 ZANU-WTF:Zimbabwe’s governmentproposedto postpone elections, extend the term of 83-year-old President Emmerson “The Crocodile” Mnangagwa, and make presidents selected by lawmakers rather than elected by the people.


Acting Director of National Intelligence Bill Pulte is not the man in this video of a meme-stock event in Florida in 2023 who is repeatedly slapped in the face with a lurid green sex toy. He is the man presented with a trophy emblazoned with “Bill Pulte fucks” and “only the young,” of which he says, “That looks pretty badass” and “I like ‘only the young,’” before dropping the trophy and saying, “I got too excited there.”

Really.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.
